decision_scenario: You need bread for the week. Based on everything you've seen — search results, listing title formats, and the full product page with competitors visible — which bread do you add to cart and what would make you choose Protein Chef over a cheaper or more familiar alternative?

rounds.stimulus: You open Blinkit to order bread. The search results show four options side by side. Option A: 'Lo! Keto Almond Bread' — thumbnail shows a dark dense loaf, price ₹149, 3.9 stars. Option B: 'Protein Chef 38g Protein Bread' — thumbnail shows a golden sliced loaf with a protein bar graphic overlay, price ₹99, 4.2 stars. Option C: 'Britannia Brown Bread' — familiar blue packaging, ₹45, 4.4 stars. Option D: 'Harvest Gold Multigrain' — ₹52, 4.3 stars. You have 10 seconds before your toddler demands attention. Which do you tap — and why?

You're now looking at three versions of the Protein Chef listing on Blinkit. Version A title: 'Protein Chef High Protein Bread — 38g Protein Per Loaf, No Maida'. Version B title: 'Double Protein Brown Bread — 6g Protein Per Slice vs 3g Regular'. Version C title: 'Protein Chef — Gym Recovery Bread, Whey + Whole Wheat, No Palm Oil'. Same price (₹99), same thumbnail, same rating (4.2 stars). Which title makes you most likely to tap through to the full listing?

You've tapped through to the Protein Chef listing. The description says: 'Each slice delivers 6g protein — equivalent to one egg. Made with whey protein concentrate, whole wheat, and zero palm oil. No maida. Shelf life 3 days (no preservatives).' There's a 'Subscribe and Save 10%' button. Four sponsored competitor ads appear at the bottom: Yoga Bar Protein Bread (₹129), BOLD Foods Protein Loaf (₹89), Slurrp Farm Multigrain (₹79), Britannia NutriProtein (₹65). What's your final decision — buy Protein Chef, tap a competitor, or exit?
rounds.decision: I'm adding Protein Chef to cart — one pack, no subscription. This is a trial purchase, not a commitment. I'm treating this the way I approached Arjun's preschool decision: gather real evidence first, then decide on continuity. The ingredient transparency earns them a first try. The 3-day shelf life is actually a feature, not a warning — it tells me there are no preservatives masking slow degradation. I will personally check the ingredient list when it arrives against what's claimed online. I'll watch how my daughter responds to the taste and texture across the week. If this clears my verification, Protein Chef becomes my standard. But I'm not delegating that judgment to a subscription algorithm — not yet, not without my own assessment first. Britannia stays in my consideration as a backup if this doesn't pass muster, but it's no longer the default.
